sentences.text: Irene Voros.
Irene is spelled I-R-E-N-E.
Voros is V-O-R-O-S.
Irene-- well, really it was Irene Kempfner.
I-R-E-N-E K-E-M-P-F-N-E-R.
And
False names?
Irene Szekely.
Spell it?
I-R-E-N S-Z-E-K-E-L-Y.
Reni, R-E-N-I.
My mother thought Irene was too formal, and so she transposed one of the letters.
January 2nd, 1937.
Vienna, Austria.
Vienna?
[LAUGHS] V-I-E-N-N-A, A-U-S-T-R-I-A.
When I came to America, they asked me if it was Australia.
No.
I was somewhere between one and a half and two, not quite two.
Yes, because they've-- they sensed the unrest because of Hitler.
And they, along with my other relatives, tried to find a place where they thought they would get away from Hitler.
Not really.
I really don't think so.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No.
Yes.
My uncle, my-- my father's brother, went to France.
My aunt went to Albania.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My other-- my-- my mother's brother went to Czechoslovakia.
They stayed in Vienna.
Hungary, Budapest.
Yes, my father was a Hungarian citizen.
And he had some connections.
He was going to start a hat factory there.
And they never dreamed that that would not be a safe place for them.
No, my grandparents were deceased.
And everybody else was in Austria prior to leaving in '38.
No, I had an aunt, actually.
Oh yes, I had an aunt.
So I did have some relatives in-- not an aunt, a cousin.
We called her aunt, that's why I mixed up.
But our cousins were in Hungary.
Budapest, the Buda side.
My mother was looking for a job because it was difficult to-- to find a job.
And she went to a boarding school.
Her profession was a nursery school teacher.
And in waiting for my mother, there was this little girl.
And she, she, she had parsley soup and she spit the parsley into my soup, and I spit it back into her soup, and we had a wonderful game of it.
And that's about as far back as I can recall.
At first, when-- when we arrived in Hungary, I think-- and I'm not real clear about this-- but I think it was at that time that my mother-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] no, that's not right either.
They lived somewhere in some apartment that I-- I don't know about.
And after that, it is that my mother worked in the boarding school.

And after my father's hat factory failed, that he couldn't find any jobs because things were getting difficult in Hungary, that he worked as a-- as a bellhop in a-- in a hotel and mother had to work in a boarding school.
And this was before I was two years old.
His name was-- [?
Vily ?] was his nickname.
Vilmos, V-I-L-M-O-S, Kempfner.
He came from Pecs originally.
His mother remarried and so he left at an early age to go to Austria to join his older sister, who was married.
And in Austria, he had a little women's store eventually.
That was quite successful.
But then a kind of a chain came around and it wasn't as successful.
And so by the time he left Vienna, he gave the store to his younger brother, who worked there with him.
OK, he edited a literary magazine in Pecs where he came from in Hungary.
And he wrote poetry, he translated poetry, and wrote short stories in this literary magazine.
He liked the theater, he liked to hang around with ladies from the theater.
And his friends were authors, you know.
He liked to read.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He had friends in the literary circles.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That was the early-- you're asking about at this point in time.
Yeah, I would say those were his interests.
Early being?
Before the war and all that.
I like to have him read to me.
He was very busy, but he-- he-- he-- he would read to me.
We went on hikes as a family, and we enjoyed that.
He went with his friends and I sort of tagged along.
I remember picking wild strawberries as the grown-ups were hurrying on and picking the strawberries and finding my own fun.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I forgot the question.
[LAUGHS]
With my dad, yeah, and this is before the war, yeah.
And I-- I remember, I think that was a little later.
I tried to write poetry like my dad and I didn't succeed very well.
She was a very outgoing person.
She had a good sense of humor.
She was strict.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She had a strict mother, sort of impatient mother, so she was impatient with me sometimes.
She had two language degrees to teach English and French.
She took the Montessori training in-- in Europe.
She was well read, she loved music, opera, in particular.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She was very well liked by people.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I guess that's about.
No, I don't remember any holidays being celebrated.
And I checked with my cousin about his sister, my dad's sister was his mother.
There was no religious celebration in my father's family.
In other words, my father's father and mother were not very religious either.
On the other hand, my mother came from a family that was religious.
Her father was a very [SIGHS] orthodox philanthropic kind of person, always gave to charities.
And he brought my mother up religiously.
But my mother sort of rebelled against it, I think.
She told me that one day she tried to bring home a Christmas tree branch because she just liked it, and he made her throw it away.
So nothing like that could be in the house.
But as his-- my grandfather's children got older, he relaxed and he let them not, you know, be as religious.
And yet my mother was always very moved by synagogues and churches.
And she brought with her from all her-- all the places she had to flee from, from Austria, from Hungary, she brought her prayer book.
And I still have the prayer book.
She got confirmed, I have her confirmation bracelet.
She wrote a poem to my uncle for his bar mitzvah.
So she was raised religiously, but I was not.
I was under two.
No other child was as young as I was.
And [SIGHS] she told me stories of-- and I guess I faintly remember.
She taught 'em a song about the washerwoman and the kids would pretend they were washing.
And they put me in a washing basket and I just sat there and they thought I was very cute.
And as I grew a little bit older, I got all the parts in the plays that my mother put on, big parts.
And I remember, when my dad came, I'd waved to him from, from the stage.
But I also remember feeling terrible because I couldn't be near my mother because she had to take care of other-- other children.
And I would whine and I would cry and they would make me feel like it was very inappropriate.
And the only time I could be with my mother was at night.
But of course, she was exhausted.
And so there wasn't very much time for me in that, in that kind of setting, and I think it affected me very much.
Yes.
Doing-- in the hotel doing the-- either he was a bellhop or he was an elevator operator.
But I know he worked in a hotel in some kind of a menial job.
I don't know, except he came up to see the plays.
So I remember that.
But I don't know.
I suppose my mother had days off, but I don't recall that.
Yes, it was in that area called Margitsziget-- Margaret Island.
It was very pretty.
I would imagine they were Jewish, but I don't know.
I could find [LAUGHS] out, but I don't know.
I think until I was-- I'm not sure of this-- but I think until I was around four years old.
My father got an apartment in Buda and we lived-- and we lived there.
I know that it was prior to the war, I know I went to first grade, you know, in Hungary living in that building.
Mm-hmm.
Yeah, you come in and on your left, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SOBBING] there was a room that was supposed to be my room.
At first, they rented that to somebody to get extra money.
But it was my room and it's not really made for a child.
It was just-- it was a room with built-in bed and built-in cupboards.
And then if you went a little further straight, then there was the kitchen.
The kitchen had-- when I left, and I don't know when, when this was put on-- but it had wall paper that was rolled on and it was grapes.
It was very pretty.
And there was a pantry to the right.
And then if you come out of the kitchen, you go down the hall a little bit, there was a-- what was supposed to be a dining room, but it was turned into a bedroom.
And my mother and I slept there.
There was a glass door dividing it from the living room.
And there was a balcony, and my dad slept there.
I don't know, I know the piano was on this side.
We lived on the fifth floor, I believe.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That's about, that's about it.
I, well, that comes later.
I-- I don't remember, you know, when I was four or after the war, I guess.
I was just going to tell you an after-the-war experience, but I realized [LAUGHS] it doesn't fit in at this point.
No, wait, no-- no, let's go back.
I had, I went to first grade and I walked to school with, with friends.
And I had one special friend that I named my daughter after.
But I remember these friends and I remember my teasing them every time somebody dropped off to their street, they started talking about that person.
And I said, it's a good thing I live at the end of the street because you can't talk about me.
So they were semi-friends, and then I had this very good friend.
OK, see, I'm mixing this up.
This is after the war what I'm telling you.
This is first grade, it's after the war.
My good friend was Christian.
No, this is after the war.
I got mixed up.
[INTERPOSING VOICES]
So we're still on before the war, right?
So I don't remember [LAUGHS] anything, other than my mother and my father.
I knew that.
See, this is why I'm having trouble with this.
I know that there was discrimination because I wore a yellow star.
So you know and I, I went to Hebrew school and they told us-- I didn't like going to Hebrew school, but this I liked.
When we went there, they told us to be proud of wearing a star.
And I remember distinctly walking down the street like this and feeling proud of my star because I was proud to be Jewish, so.
Probably not four, kids didn't go to Hebrew school, I don't think, when they were four, probably five.
And also, just to interrupt, the story I told you before is pre-war.
I did go to first grade before the war, so that is, you know, that is correct.
I don't know about my best friend, I think that was after the war.
Yeah.
Yes, yes, I was in first grade.
Yes, it was a public school.
Yes, but I don't know if it was discrimination against me as a Jew.
I-- I remember distinctly-- I went back to Hungary many years later and I stood on the playground where we were.
And I remember distinctly kids playing in a circle and me standing on the outside and feeling totally left out.
But I don't know if I was left out as a person or left out as a Jew.
I don't remember when I wore the yellow star.
Probably yes, because I went to school until we no longer could go to school.
So yes, I must have worn the yellow star to school.
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] I don't remember anything being told to me other than that.
There was a point in time when my mother sent me away to a-- a border town between Hungary and Yugoslavia.
And I-- I-- I definitely had the feeling that it was unsafe to be in, in, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what was in Hungary, in Budapest.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I-- I was prepped to go there.
I-- I-- I was taught the Catholic prayer, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] some other things about, you know, being a Christian so that I could pass as a Christian.
And I was sent to live with-- with some peasants in-- in this, in this border town.
So I don't remember anybody explaining to me why this is happening except that it's dangerous to be here.
I went there on a train.
Probably with my mother, probably my mother took me there.
I'm not-- I don't recall that, because I remember coming back, and I came back by myself, and she met me at the train station.
So I might have been just put on the train and then received there by these people, I don't remember.
Probably not.
Probably this is the time he was in work camp.
This is probably how I found out it was dangerous because at one point my dad was packing his-- his things and he was crying.
And you know, there was a big to do.
And he-- he was sent to work camp and we didn't know if we'd see him again or not.
So was a-- you know, as a child, I must have perceived that this was a very, you know, threatening situation.
And I think maybe that's how my mother perceived that it's time to get me out because this is, this is happening.
Do you remember the name of the border town you were in?
No, all I know it was on the border of Yugoslavia and Hungary.
Well, somewhere between five and six.
No, that's not right either, because I went to first grade.
Somewhere between six and seven, maybe.
You know, I'm not good at the dates.
[INTERPOSING VOICES]
Could be.
I really think that the, the reason that I'm so unclear about dates is because I've been, I've been told to lie about my age and, and deliberately cloud over things.
And ever since then, I, I am not good with-- with dates, that they're just a sore point with me because all reality has been turned upside down.
You know, I'm not six, you know, I'm five, I'm not six.
I'm not Jewish, I'm Catholic.
So everything got sort of turned tipsy-turvy on me.
And I think some of that has-- has remained.
So I always have to think back.
Even about my own age, [LAUGHS] I have to think back.
Because my mother was afraid that if I went to public school there, I'd give away my Jewishness.
And it was very dangerous to be Jewish, as I found out.
One of my most harrowing experiences was playing with the village kids.
And I think it's because I was wearing something different, they started yelling at me and they started calling me Jew.
And I ran like crazy, my heart was pounding.
And I thought I was surely going to die because they knew who I was.
And I ran, you know, to the home that I was living in.
And only later did I realize as an adult that they didn't know that I was Jewish, they only knew I was different.
But it was very, very frightening for me.
So it was dangerous to be Jewish.
And it was, it was, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was not good to be put in a position where you can give away who you really were.
I had some other harrowing [LAUGHS] experiences, but they were sort of funny.
The first time this peasant couple took me to church, I was so bored because the masses were in Latin.
I walked in with a dress down below my knees and I walked out with the dress down to my ankles because I proceeded to open up all the seams because I didn't know what to do with myself.
So these people surely know that I was Jewish.
My mother wrote me a letter and she sent me a crucifix.
And in the letter, she explains this is a crucifix, you use it to pray with, and so on.
I opened up the letter and I was just delighted.
And I said, oh, a necklace and I put it on.
So these people surely knew that I was Jewish.
You know, the ones that-- they were being paid to keep me, but they could have given me away if, you know, if they wanted to.
Of course, I was their source of income, so, but.
Do you recall their names?
No, no.
It was a peasant house.
We all slept in the same bed.
All the children slept in the same bed.
The father would come home drunk and we'd all tremble and be afraid because we didn't know what he would do.
But we were just-- it was just the bedroom.
Most likely the bedroom and the kitchen area was what it contained.
I don't remember being treated badly.
I was treated probably like the rest of the kids-- ignored.
We-- we-- we played, we, you know, we played outside and made up our own games and things.
I, I'm sure I was treated like the other children.
[SIGHS] I think basically I was very angry at my mother for sending me away and for abandoning me in a place where I was totally defenseless.
And that came out later on when my mother sent for me 'cause things were getting too dangerous and she picked me up.
Instead of running into her arms and hugging her, I walked up her and I said, you're getting fat, you're really fat.
